teaching people how to use the different GTM tools.

so Loic in the call is the.

largely the platform has been built for the intention of really our team to execute and do the day-to-day operations of our accounts.

And, you know, really just leaning into more of, like, service as a software as a Play and finding, like, there's, you know, a lot of demand for that.

We find when you take that right approach and you can play around different styles, you feed in that right context, that's when you can get the high quality content.

And so we've had to really think about how do you hit that bar and how do we evolve our process?

may be an air ops even, et cetera.

So.
There's certainly a possibility, though, if there's a point in the future.

Could be blogs, but not limited to blogs.

What's resonant?

Where we think about it, we want our brands to operate, our customers to operate and think more like publishers and really build that editorial engine first and foremost and create content that's amazing answers to the questions that your personal lives have.

Quick on the team, that's very much informed the team we have and so like highly editorially focused team.

How we start our engagements is essentially off of what we call a strategy sprint.
I can show you one that's in progress real quick just to give you some insight into the deliverables.
But essentially what we're doing is we're creating a lot of those AI-ready artifacts.
We're going really deep with you to develop the roadmap and just making sure we're prescribing exactly what you need.
Essentially, it's doing this kind of work is what enables us to go super fast.
We find that if you skip past this and you take too many shortcuts, that's where it becomes more challenging to get to repeatable outcomes.
So we're finding this is like really resonating with our customers.
So far we have 100% conversion out of a strategy sprint because the way we structure this is essentially there's no obligation to work with us beyond this point.
You can take a lot of these artifacts, run with it, et cetera, but that's not the intention why we do these.
It's more to de-risk the long-term engagement and for us to have high confidence.

And typically these range in like six to 12 months, you know, average strategy sprints typically be like 20 to 30k for that eight week period, you know, on this side for like growth execution or for like really scaling content consistently, really can range depending on your needs of content on the low end, it can be around 9k on the high end 25 plus, usually when it's 25 plus though, we're doing like 15 plus editorial pieces a week, lots of programmatic pages, custom workflows, just, you know, kind of the kitchen sink, if you will, of a lot of different things.
Just to jump forward to like a little bit of like what we actually do during the strategy sprint.

perspective, very high level, but there's a bit of depth in here of like, just like, you know, really digging into your personas, we're big fans of the jobs to be done framework, and really digging to that and understanding that.

What does that definition have done with you?
And what is that actual monthly amount that makes sense?

the execution phase, that's also when you start running outbound for the clients then, right?

is developing that content either editorially or programmatic-based content that drives those eyeballs, and then we can experiment with other things, but I could probably count on one hand outbound things we've done, and we've purposefully kind of said, hey, we don't want to take those on.

I know we have like only a few minutes left, but something we'd want to discuss with you is that if you look at DeepGram, which is like one of the case study on the websites, and I think that has happened with a lot of companies that went through like AI SEO, so like a lot of companies using arops, they usually have like the same graph that we see here, where you see like a massive like uptick in traffic, and then it usually crashed down right after So like, how do you avoid this?

Because we see that a lot, we are working with arops very closely, and we see that with a lot of tech clients, where it's like usually you see like a massive uptick at the beginning, when you start running out a lot of pages, but then months later, I don't know if it's Google that's kind of catching up, but then you get penalized.
So what's your trick to avoid this?
Yeah, so this is like one I would say, you know, not obviously the first time you've heard that question.
And like, it's not it.
Well, that is part of like, kind of where this journey.
It is so different to what we do today versus what Marcel was executing back then in the deep grand days.
Essentially, that was super, super heavy programmatic.
They scaled out a ton of pages.
And if you look at Clay, for example, even Clay actually went through a similar journey.
They had a big spike.
They put out a ton of programmatic pages.
They got dinged about a couple months back.
Looks like they're recovering.
But just leaning way too heavy on just pure programmatic and thinner content.
And basically, what happened after Marcel left, they stopped enriching those pages.
They stopped investing in them.
They stopped continuing to refresh them.
And they started to get dinged as a result.
For us, especially brands of your phase, we would rarely recommend leaning heavy programmatic and first starting with the editorial layer first, developing that topical authority.
And think of it as like building that muscle where then we can experiment with programmatic on top.
And so that's not a hard, fast rule, but generally that's our mind.
If you came in and we saw an opportunity for thousands of thousands of pages one day we could create for you, amazing.
That could be a fruitful opportunity.
That doesn't mean that's where we're going to recommend to start from day one and then start to experiment to build into that, right?
So first, build that topical authority where you're not going to take on that same risk.
Then we start, you know, for Vappy, we did this, right?
Like we identified 3,000 plus pages.
We created a pretty cool, you know, I'll show you real quick of programmatic pages for them.
We identified these different use cases.
We helped build these pages.
We helped create the system prompts with our workflows, set this whole strategy, template, et cetera.

What we found, I think it was like 3,500 use cases that we could scale out and show their product in a cool way, which is you can literally interact with this and it talks about managing cancellations.
And so we just saw it as like a pretty cool opportunity to scale.
We started with 100 pages.
We looked at like how quickly are those pages getting indexed?
Are they getting high click through?
Are they getting engagement, et cetera?
And it was super, super strong early on.
And then we started to open it up.
And so there's just different ways of thinking about this.

You just have to be mindful of if you just launch 10,000 plus pages or whatever in a very short period of time, you likely are going to see one of those graphs eventually because you just are trying to shortcut too much.
And so that's something we very rarely would ever recommend to anyone going that fast and doing that type of velocity.
While we have the capability of doing that, you have to take the longer vision in mind.
And so I'll be happy to share specific customers later that are more of those steady growths.
Not that there is an accelerated growth, but it is continual growth, right?
It isn't just that up and then down.
can crash it to your point.

break it down.

we can.

and what you guys are doing.
Yeah, you know, we, our journey started there.
You know, we started building in there.
we found was just, while it's a great tool, we found some challenges of any time that you need to change something, swap in a new model, change your workflow in some way.
There was a lot of refactoring and time that went to starting to adapt those workflows.
So over time, we started to build our own workflow engine, even when we were working with their ops and we were pinging through the API.
It just enabled us to move faster.
Essentially, we were just managing inputs and outputs then with air ops.
And we were finding there was a lot of things we wanted to add feature-wise.
So essentially, the way I would frame it to answer your question more directly is, you know, they're a bit more of a pure software play.
They'll help typically brands from our experience, like, get off the ground and build workflows.
But usually when it gets to, you know, a few months in, it's like, okay, what happens next?
I don't have time to, like, continue to, like, build out these workflows and things of that nature.
And that's essentially been our experiences.
Well, for a lot of brands we've talked to, it's just, they just need more help.
Like they don't have time to fiddle around with a bunch of workflows.
They don't have the experience or capacity.

And they just much prefer us to help them build the right strategy and move super fast and still do some of the same elements of what the outcomes and the inspiration is behind AirOps, but just do it from more of a managed services angle.
And when you guys started the agency, you were actually implementing AirOps for clients.
And then you guys were like, okay, AirOps is not good enough.
Let us build our own tech to actually deliver the service, basically.
Yeah, I would say that's a summary, yeah.
And nothing against them, you know, like I think for a lot of folks, like it can be a great tool, like, you know, I love playing around with things in there.
It's just in the kind of scale and demands that we need to create for it, just necessitated us to build in a different way.
Oh, perfect.
Yeah, I would love to explore a bit more, Kyle.
So if we can do like a follow-up meeting would be ideal.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, how's a Monday or Tuesday for y'all next week?
I could do, yeah, but this is going to be, what are you based, East Coast or West Coast?
I'm in Pacific time.
So, yeah, you're probably dealing with some pretty different time zones, I'm guessing.
I can do late Monday, Monday nights.
I know if you, for works for you guys.
For worse case, like, we can also record it.
Yeah, yeah, cool.
I have 10 to 11 Pacific, I have 12 to 2 Pacific.
I don't know what the math is, but I can also just, like, I could send you all a link if you just want to find time for early next week.
Maybe that's easiest.
let's do that, and then we will coordinate internally and get back to you then.
All right, sounds like a plan.
Looking forward to it, guys.
Perfect.
Thanks a lot, Kyle, for your time.
Have a nice one.
Have a good one.
Take care.
Take care.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introductory call between ColdIQ and GrowthX to explore potential AI SEO collaboration and content scaling strategies.](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [ColdIQ seeks to scale SEO efforts, particularly with AI-driven content creation](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
  - [GrowthX offers a comprehensive strategy-first approach to content scaling, focusing on editorial content before programmatic](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [GrowthX's process involves an initial 8-week strategy sprint ($20-30k) followed by ongoing execution ($9-25k+/month)](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1175.0)
  - [GrowthX emphasizes sustainable growth over rapid, potentially risky content scaling](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1591.0)

## Topics

### ColdIQ's Business Model and Goals

  - [Multi-product company: agency (GTM systems), info products, and tool directory](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=257.0)
  - [Primary revenue streams: outbound system setup, AI sales agency training, tool partnerships](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [Main goal: Increase qualified website traffic across all products](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=497.0)
  - [Current success: 85% of meetings booked from LinkedIn organic content](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=525.0)

### GrowthX's Approach and Services

  - [Focus on building editorial engines for clients, prioritizing long-form content](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=925.0)
  - [Team includes experienced editors and SEO experts (e.g., ex-TechCrunch, G2)](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1049.0)
  - [Strategy sprint: 8-week process to create AI-ready artifacts and develop content roadmap](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1094.0)
  - [Execution phase: Consistent content publishing based on strategy (15+ pieces/week for larger engagements)](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1108.0)
  - [Custom platform for workflow management and content creation](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1676.0)

### AI SEO Strategies and Challenges

  - [GrowthX avoids rapid, large-scale programmatic content creation to prevent potential penalties](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1430.0)
  - [Emphasis on building topical authority through editorial content before exploring programmatic options](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1491.0)
  - [Careful approach to scaling: start small, monitor performance, then expand gradually](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1555.0)
  - [Focus on continual content refreshment and enrichment to maintain growth](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1663.0)

### GrowthX vs. Other Solutions (e.g., AirOps)

  - [GrowthX evolved from using AirOps to developing their own platform](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1742.0)
  - [Offers more hands-on, managed service compared to pure software solutions](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1784.0)
  - [Custom workflow engine allows for faster adaptations and feature additions](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1764.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Schedule a follow-up 45-minute meeting for early next week](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1852.0)
  - [Kyle to send a scheduling link for the team to find a suitable time](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1895.0)
  - [Deeper dive into GrowthX's platform, use cases, and customer examples in the next meeting](https://fathom.video/share/2o-vrn56HyycbSYQnaHasQtr3z8Kbm-e?tab=summary&timestamp=1717.0)
